observations: Clean separation: extract_windows_rg_bundle is a standalone function with a single responsibility.
WINDOWS_RG_DIRNAME constant eliminates magic string duplication between the extraction function and resolve_rg_binary.
The fallback chain in resolve_rg_binary is logical and clearly ordered: PATH -> existing extraction -> auto-extract -> error.
Good use of next() iterator with default for safe member lookup in the zip archive.
Minimal diff (48 lines added, 2 changed across 2 files) — follows the 'smallest defensible change' guideline from AGENTS.md.
The auto-extraction is correctly gated behind platform.system() == 'Windows', matching the Windows-specific rg.zip bundle contents.
The resolve_rg_binary fallback chain preserves backward compatibility: PATH lookup first, then pre-existing extraction, then auto-extract.
extract_windows_rg_bundle correctly handles edge cases: missing archive (returns None), missing rg.exe member in archive (returns None), extraction failure (returns None).
Verified against actual rg.zip contents — the archive does contain 'ripgrep-14.1.0-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc/rg.exe' matching the expected path.
The error message in FileNotFoundError was updated to mention the rg.zip extraction path, aiding debuggability.
Worker verified end-to-end: ran benchmarks/run_benchmarks.py with rg absent from PATH and all 10 parity rows passed.

test_run_benchmarks_should_extract_windows_rg_zip_when_rg_missing properly creates a fake rg.zip, monkeypatches __file__, shutil.which, and platform.system, and asserts both correct path resolution and file content.
TDD approach was followed: test was written first and verified failing before the implementation was made.
Missing test: no explicit test for the case where rg.zip exists but does NOT contain rg.exe (the None-return early exit). This is a non-blocking gap since the function handles it correctly.
Missing test: no test verifying the non-Windows platform path (where extraction is skipped). Again non-blocking since the platform gate is straightforward.

nonBlockingIssues.description: bundle.extractall(benchmarks_dir) extracts all zip contents without path validation. While the zip is a first-party repository bundle (not user-supplied), Python 3.12+ supports extractall(path, filter='data') for zip-slip protection. Consider adding path traversal checks if this pattern is reused for untrusted archives.
No test for the edge case where rg.zip exists but doesn't contain an rg.exe entry. The code handles this correctly (returns None then raises FileNotFoundError) but a test would document the behavior.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker couldn't execute .factory/init.sh on Windows and ran 'uv pip install -e .[dev,ast,nlp]' directly. This is a known issue documented in mission AGENTS.md ('`.factory/init.sh` not executable on native Windows'). The skillFeedback.followedProcedure was false due to this and the PYTHONPATH workaround. Both deviations are justified by documented pre-existing issues.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.deviations[0]: 'Ran the equivalent editable install command' because init.sh path resolution failed on Windows.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature description mentioned adding a benchmark setup command to .factory/services.yaml as an alternative approach, but the worker chose auto-extraction within the script itself. This is arguably a better design since it requires no manual setup and is fully transparent. However, services.yaml was not updated to document the auto-extraction behavior.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: services.yaml still only has 'benchmark_text: python benchmarks/run_benchmarks.py' without noting the auto-extraction capability.

summary: The implementation correctly teaches benchmarks/run_benchmarks.py to auto-extract benchmarks/rg.zip on Windows when rg is missing from PATH. The code is clean, minimal, and well-tested. The fallback chain (PATH -> pre-extracted binary -> auto-extract from zip -> error) is logical and preserves backward compatibility. The worker followed TDD, ran all local gates (lint, typecheck, tests, benchmarks), and verified end-to-end functionality in an isolated worktree. No blocking issues found.
